coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of stable and biocompatible red fluorescent glycopolymer nanoparticles for cellular imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Meiying
Wang, Ke
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Zhang, Xiqi
Li, Zhen
Zhang, Qingsong
Huang, Zengfang
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Glycosylated cross-linked red fluorescent amphiphilic polymer has been facilely synthesized in one pot, which would readily self-assemble into nanoparticles with high water dispersibility in aqueous media. The resulted nanoparticles have surplus carboxyl groups and glycosyl groups on the surface, which can be further functionalized, and they can demonstrate strong bio-favorable red fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield of 18% due to the aggregation induced emission (AIE) dyes aggregated in the core. Moreover, the nanoparticles also revealed great photostability and structure stability with an ultralow critical micelle concentration of 0.0018 mg mL−1. Their excellent biocompatibility and cell uptake behavior make them promising for cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation induced emission
Red fluorescence
Glycopolymer nanoparticles
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of PhE2-ITA-Glu copolymer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectrum of PhE2-ITA-Glu showing the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur.
(A) 1H NMR spectra of PhE2 and PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs. (B) FTIR spectra of PhE2, ITA, Glu, and PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs.
(A) TEM image of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs. (B) Dynamic light scattering results of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs in PBS.
(A) The UV absorption spectra of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs dispersed in water. (B) The fluorescent spectra of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs dispersed in water (λex=470 nm).
Intensity of the aggregate emission versus logarithm of the concentration of PhE2-ITA-Glu (λex=488 nm and λem=605 nm).

Biocompatibility evaluation of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs. (A–C) optical microscopy images of A549 cells incubated with different concentrations of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs for 24 h, (A) control cells, (B) 40 μg mL−1, (C) 80 μg mL−1, (D) cell viability of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs with A549 cells for 8 and 24 h, respectively.

CLSM images of A549 cells. (A) Differential interference contrast image; (B) the cell nuclei were stained by Hoechst 33258 with the excitation wavelength of 405 nm; (C) fluorescent image taken after incubation with 20 μg mL−1 of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs for 3 h under the excitation wavelength of 458 nm; (D) the corresponding overlay image. Scale bar=20 μm.
Schematic showing the preparation of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of stable and biocompatible red fluorescent glycopolymer nanoparticles for cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Polymers, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology (Ministry of Education), Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Bio-Inspired Smart Interface Science, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Bio-Inspired Smart Interface Science
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glycosylated cross-linked red fluorescent amphiphilic polymer has been facilely synthesized in one pot, which would readily self-assemble into nanoparticles with high water dispersibility in aqueous media. The resulted nanoparticles have surplus carboxyl groups and glycosyl groups on the surface, which can be further functionalized, and they can demonstrate strong bio-favorable red fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield of 18% due to the aggregation induced emission (AIE) dyes aggregated in the core. Moreover, the nanoparticles also revealed great photostability and structure stability with an ultralow critical micelle concentration of 0.0018 mg mL−1. Their excellent biocompatibility and cell uptake behavior make them promising for cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellular fluorescence imaging has captured great attention as a versatile visualization way for medical diagnosis, drug development, and clinical study. 1–7 Therefore, various fluorescent bioprobes have been extensively studied over the past decades, such as fluorescent protein, semiconductor quantum dots, rare earth-doped upconversion nanoparticles, and organic dye-doped nanoparticles. 8–13 Among these diverse fluorescent probes, red emissive bioprobes were more attractive in the depth imaging and tracing applications, because the red fluorescence was far away from the bio-autofluorescence emission range and can penetrate deeper into the biology systems to obtain images with high contrast and resolution. 14–16 However, fluorescent protein often encounters the problem of poor photostability, small stokes shifts, and tedious transfection process. 17 While both inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and rare earth-doped upconversion nanoparticles suffer from the high cytotoxicity due to the containing of heavy metal ions, which severely restricts their real biomedical applications. 18–20 For most organic dyes-doped nanoparticles, the hydrophobic planar structures of dyes will induce strong intermolecular π–π interactions, leading to fluorescence quenching and photobleaching, which was especially serious for red fluorescent molecules. 21–23 To conquer this aggregation-caused quenching problem, another type of unique organic dyes were developed by Tang et al. in 2001, which can emit much stronger luminescence in their aggregation states and were called aggregation induced emission (AIE) dyes. 22,24 After that a large number of AIE fluorogens and these AIE fluorogens based fluorescent polymer nanoparticles have been rapidly developed and extensively investigated for chemosensors and bioimaging applications by now. 25–37 Nevertheless, as many aforementioned AIE based fluorescent polymers nanoparticles are formed by self-assembling of linear polymers, which are not stable in dilute solution below the critical micelle concentration (CMC). 38–40 In this case, cross-linked polymeric nanoparticles are expected to be more stable in the practical applications. Therefore, preparation of novel stable cross-linked fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles based on AIE dyes is of great scientific interest, and more versatile strategies are still highly demanded. 41

In order to endow the AIE based polymer with favorable compatibility for bioapplications, biomass monomers seem to be very attractive for the construction of fluorescent polymer nanoparticles. Glucose is an ideal candidate, which is frequently utilized in the fabrication of biomedical polymers. For examples, glucose-containing polymers demonstrated huge potential for various tissue engineering and biomedical applications, since they can be facilely functionalized as therapeutic drugs, drug delivery vehicles, cell culture matrices and biocompatible materials. 39,42–44 In addition, carbohydrates at the cell surface play important roles in diverse biological recognition events owning to the specific reactions of glucose with proteins and other biological entities (e.g., cells, pathogens). 45–48 In order to connect glucose monomer with AIE monomer, itaconic anhydride (ITA) is a good cross-linker since ITA can be facilely polymerized and is reactive with amide compounds. Moreover, ITA is also very economic and benign to environment. Therefore, by combining with these two biomass monomers together, fluorescent glycopolymer nanoparticles (FGNs) prepared using ITA and AIE monomers would become promising biomaterials for tracing and medical applications.

Herein, stable red florescent glycopolymer based on AIE dyes (PhE2-ITA-Glu) was facilely synthesized via free radical polymerization and ring-opening reaction from AIE monomer (PhE2), biomass monomer itaconic anhydride (ITA), and glucosamine hydrochloride (Glu) for the first time. The obtained PhE2-ITA-Glu amphiphilic polymer was readily self-assemble into stable nanoparticles owning to their cross-linked structures, and the surplus glycosyl groups covered on the surface of these FGNs endowed them with great water dispersibility. More importantly, the FGNs demonstrated intense bio-favorable red fluorescence with high fluorescence quantum yield and great photostability thanks to the red AIE dyes aggregated in the core. Furthermore, the biocompatibility and cell uptake behavior of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs were also determined to evaluate their potential cellular imaging applications (Scheme 1 ).

Phenothiazine, 1-bromooctadecane, 1,2-dichloroethane, phosphoryl chloride, 2-(4- bromophenyl) acetonitrile, 4-vinylphenylboronic acid, Aliquat 336, tetrakis(triphenylphosphine) palladium(0), tetrabutylammonium hydroxide, N,N- dimethylacetamide (DMAc), azobisisobutyronitrile purchased from Alfa Aesar were used as received. Itaconic anhydride and glucosamine hydrochloride were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. and used as received. All of other agents were obtained from commercial sources and used as received. Ultra-pure water was used through the whole experiments.

The chemical structures were characterized by 1H NMR spectroscopy and FTIR spectroscopy with a JEOL 400 MHz spectrometer and a Perkin–Elmer Spectrum 100 spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA) in transmission mode, respectively. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analyses were performed on a Shimadzu LC-20AD pump system. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were studied by a VGESCALAB 220-IXL spectrometer using an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The optical properties were characterized with UV/Vis/NIR Perkin–Elmer lambda750 spectrometer and PE LS-55 spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). The morphology and size distribution of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images using a JEM-1200EX microscope and a zeta Plus apparatus (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY). The detailed conditions were the same with those described in our previous reports.

The AIE monomer PhE2 was synthesized according to the literature methods. 49 In order to prepare PhE2-ITA-Glu, firstly PhE2 was cross-linked with ITA through free radical polymerization, then ring-opening reaction of ITA by Glu was facilely conducted at room temperature under air atmosphere, affording the amphiphilic polymer (Scheme 2 ). To be specific, PhE2 (36.4 mg, 0.040 mmol), ITA (45 mg, 0.40 mmol), and AIBN (5.0 mg) were dispersed in 6 mL ethyl acetate first. The above mixture was put into a schlenk tube and purged with nitrogen flow for about 30 min. Then the tube was set into an oil bath of 80 °C for 12 h under stirring. Glu (86 mg, 0.40 mmol) was dispersed in 6 mL of methanol, to which five drops of triethylamine were added, and stirred at 60 °C for 12 h. Then Glu solution in methanol was added to the above tube, being stirred for another 2 h at room temperature. Afterwards, the reaction was ended and the solution was dialyzed using 7000 Da Mw cutoff dialysis membranes against tap water and ethanol for 24 h and 6 h, respectively. Finally, the solution in dialysis bag was freeze-dried to afford the product (Scheme 2).

Human lung adenocarcinoma epithelial (A549) cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Culture medium was changed every two days in order to keep the exponential growth of the cells.

The effects of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs to A549 cells can be examined by checking the changes of cell morphology and number. Firstly, cells were seeded in 6-well microplates with a density of 7×104 cells mL−1 in 2 mL culture media. After cell attachment, different concentrations of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs prepared in culture media were employed to incubate with A549 cells at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, all samples were carefully washed with PBS for three times to remove the uninternalized nanoparticles, and the cell morphology was observed utilizing an optical microscopy (Leica, Germany) with the magnification of ×10.

Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay was used to evaluate the cell viability of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs on A549 cells based on our previous reports. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at 37 °C overnight for attachment. Then, DMEM solution containing PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs with different concentrations were added to the wells for incubation of 8 and 24 h, respectively. Subsequently, the nanoparticles were carefully removed by washing with PBS for three times, and 10 μL of CCK-8 dye together with 100 μL of DMEM culture medium were added to each well. After the plate was incubated for 2 h at 37 °C, the absorbance of each well was measured at 450 nm on a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin–Elmer).

On the day prior before experiments, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 7×104 cells per dish. After cell attachment of overnight, the cells were incubated with PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs at a final concentration of 20 μg mL−1 for 3 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the internalized PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min at room temperature. After the cells were stained with Hoechst 33258, the cells were immediately imaged by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany). The detailed information about the staining experiments was the same with those described in our previous reports. 50

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PhE2-ITA-Glu copolymer
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the cross-linked polymer, AIE dye PhE2 with two alkenyl end groups was chosen as the fluorescent component. The AIE fluorogen PhE2 was synthesized according to the synthetic route described in our previous report. Its structure was characterized and confirmed by standard spectroscopic methods. 11,51 For preparation of PhE2-ITA-Glu, PhE2 was first employed to react with ITA via free radical polymerization to get a cross-linked hydrophobic copolymer. Then PhE2-ITA copolymer reacted with Glu through ring-opening reaction at room temperature to obtain the amphiphilic glycopolymer. The set molar ratio of three monomers (PhE2, ITA, and Glu) was 1:10:10, thus the degree of polymerization (DP) was designed as 10. After the resulting PhE2-ITA-Glu copolymers were purified through dialysis, it was determined by GPC that the number average molecular weight (Mn) values of PhE2-ITA-Glu were 40,585 Da with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 1.65, which meant the DP of the copolymers could be calculated as about 10. In order to characterize the chemical composition of PhE2-ITA-Glu, the elemental information of the polymer was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) studies, which was demonstrated in Fig. 1 . It can be found that carbon and oxygen were the major components, while other two minor components were nitrogen and sulfur. To be specific, the overall wt% of elements present in PhE2-ITA-Glu was calculated as C: O: N: S∼68.8: 25.6: 5.2: 0.4. The main peak of carbon was at 284.7 eV with a shoulder peak at 286 eV, which were ascribed to the sp2 and sp3 atoms. The N 1s, O 1s and S 2p XPS spectra of PhE2-ITA-Glu showed peak at around 399.6, 532 and 163.6 eV, respectively (Fig. S1). These results demonstrated that monomers were successfully incorporated into the PhE2-ITA-Glu.

The structure of obtained PhE2-ITA-Glu copolymer was fully characterized by 1H NMR spectra and FTIR spectra. The 1H NMR spectra of PhE2 and PhE2-ITA-Glu in DMSO-d 6 were demonstrated in Fig. 2 A. Due to the free radical polymerization, the alkenyl end groups in PhE2, with the chemical shifts at around 5.2, 5.7 and 6.7 ppm, were consumed. Therefore, these chemical shifts cannot be found in PhE2-ITA-Glu anymore. However, the peaks of aromatic group around 7.7 can be observed in both PhE2 and PhE2-ITA-Glu because the polymerization did not consume the aromatic structure. The chemical shift at around 2.9 ppm indicated the successful introduction of ITA to PhE2, and the chemical shift of N–H around 8.2 ppm demonstrated that Glu was covalently attached to the main chain of polymer. Meanwhile, a chemical shift around 1.7, which came from the glycosyl groups, also clearly confirmed the successful formation of PhE2-ITA-Glu. Successful syntheses of PhE2-ITA-Glu were further verified by FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2B, two characteristic stretching vibration peaks of CH3 and CH2 groups at 2921 and 2847 cm−1, two evident stretching vibration peaks of CO band at around 1845 and 1772 cm−1, and the obvious stretching vibration peak of O–H band at 3286 cm−1 can be clearly identified from the three monomers PhE2, ITA, and Glu, respectively. After the radical polymerization and ring open reaction of the three monomers, the cross-linked glycopolymers PhE2-ITA-Glu were formed. It can be seen from the spectrum of PhE2-ITA-Glu, the broad and strong absorption of O–H band at around 3286 cm−1 and the evident stretching vibration band of C–N at 1095 cm−1 verified the successful attachment of Glu to the polymer. Furthermore, together with the CO stretching vibration at 1772 cm−1 and the stretching vibration peak of C–O at 1031 cm−1, it was confirmed that PhE2-ITA-Glu copolymer had been successful prepared.

Self-assembly of amphiphilic polymer to form nanoparticles has been a particularly facile and promising strategy for constructions of various nanomaterials. The amphiphilic cross-linked PhE2-ITA-Glu polymers were tended to self-assemble into stable nanoparticles when they were dispersed in aqueous solution because of their cross-linked structures. The surfaces of these nanoparticles were covered with many glycosyl groups, which can be used for further functionalized and endow the PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs with great water dispersibility. In order to confirm the formation of the PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs, their transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected. From Fig. 3 A, it can be clearly found that there were many spherical nanoparticles with diameters ranged from 50 to 100 nm, which directly proved that the PhE2-ITA-Glu copolymers were indeed self-assembled into nanoparticles. At the same time, a zetaPlus particle size analyzer was also employed to determine the size distribution of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The results demonstrated that the size distribution of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs was 212.0±70.6 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.005 (Fig. 3B), which was somewhat larger than the size characterized by TEM because of the hydrodynamic effects. In addition, drying-causing shrinkage of the amphiphilic copolymers may also cause the smaller size in TEM.

After the self-assembly of PhE2-ITA-Glu glycopolymer into nanoparticles, the conjugated aromatic groups with AIE characteristics were aggregated into the hydrophobic cores. Therefore, the resulted FGNs can emit intense red fluorescence. Herein, the UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs in water (pH 7.0) were studied to determine their optical properties. The UV spectra were shown in Fig. 4 A, and it could be found that there were two absorption peaks located at around 341 and 470 nm. It was found that the intensity of spectra started to increase from the very beginning, also indicating the existence of nanoparticles in the solution, which was ascribed to the Mie effect. 26

The PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs demonstrated strong red fluorescence in water with a quantum yield of about 18%, which was determined using quinine sulfate as the reference dye. The fluorescence spectra of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs in water were shown in Fig. 4B. The maximum emission wavelength was located at 605 nm, while the fluorescence excitation wavelength was around 450 nm. Furthermore, the photostability of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs was quite satisfactory with almost no fluorescence intensity decrease even after more than 25 min continuous irradiation (Fig. S2). Thanks to their great stability and intense red emission, these PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs have great potential for depth bioimaging and other biomedical applications.

In order to evaluate the potential bioimaging and biomedical applications of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs, their biocompatibility to A549 cells was determined. Firstly the influences of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs to A549 cells were examined by observing cell morphology using optical microscopy after the cells were incubated with different concentrations of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs for 24 h. Fig. 6 demonstrated that cells still adhered to the cell plate and grown quite well with the existence of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs for such a long time. Meanwhile, being compared with the control cells cultured without PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs in Fig. 4A, no obvious cell morphology change or cell number decrease were observed even when the concentration of PhE2-ITA-Glu

FGNs reached up to 80 μg mL−1 (Fig. 6B and C). Furthermore, in order to evaluate the cytocompatibility of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs quantitatively, cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay was employed to test the cell viability of FGNs to A549 cells. As shown in Fig. 6D, after the cells were incubated with 10–120 μg mL−1 of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs for 8 h and 24 h, the cell viability value was still maintained at greater than 90% even when the concentration of these FGNs was as high as 120 μg mL−1.

In view of the excellent biocompatibility, water dispersibility, stability and fluorescence property of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs, their cellular uptake behavior and cellular imaging were investigated by Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) observation to evaluate their potential biomedical applications. Hoechst 33258 was used to stain the cell nuclei for 5 min in dark before cellular imaging studies. The images were taken upon excitation at 405 nm and 458 nm while 440–500 nm and 510–700 nm bandpass filters were adopted for fluorescence collection from Hoechst 33258 and PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs, respectively. The fluorescent image of A549 cells incubated with 20 μg mL−1 of PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs for three hours was demonstrated in Fig. 7 , from which it can be evidently found that the FGNs localized in the cytoplasm of A549 cells. The intense fluorescence emission was ascribed to the aggregation of AIE dyes PhE2 in the core of these FGNs, and their great stability played important roles in the imaging applications. These images not only meant that PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs could be facilely uptaken by A549 cells, which was considered through endocytosis of the cells, but also demonstrated their excellent tracing ability. Therefore, it was reasonable to expect that the PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs should be promising candidates for cellular imaging and tracing applications with all these advantages, such as stable morphology in dilute physiological solution, intense fluorescence, good water dispersibility, and excellent biocompatibility. Moreover, in this work, ITA and Glu were used as alternative monomers for the construction of cross-linked fluorescent FGNs; various functional components can be further attached, and the introduction of Glu may endow them potential preponderance for diverse biomedical applications.

In summary, novel stable red fluorescent PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs were facilely prepared by self-assembling of cross-linked amphiphilic glycopolymer (PhE2-ITA-Glu), and they were successfully adopted for cellular imaging applications due to their numerous superb properties such as great stability, high water dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility, uniform morphology, and strong red fluorescence. The red fluorescent cross-linked glycopolymer (PhE2-ITA-Glu) was synthesized from AIE dye and biomass monomers through free radical polymerization and ring-open reaction in one-pot for the first time, which could readily self-assemble into stable PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs in pure aqueous solution. Importantly, owning to the existing of glucose on the surface of FGNs, PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs are expected to exhibit diverse properties for biomedical applications. Moreover, many other functional components such as drugs, genes, targeting agents and other imaging agents can be further integrated into PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs because of the surplus carboxyl groups attached on PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs. Therefore, multifunctional theranostic platforms based on PhE2-ITA-Glu FGNs can be fabricated. In one word, this work provided a new construction method to afford stable red fluorescent FGNs potential for cellular imaging and other biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21134004, 21201108, 51363016, 2147457), the National 973 Project (Nos. 2011CB935700), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2015M571018).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.tet.2015.06.074.
